content.text: A closure is a function that remembers the variables from the scope where it was created, even after that scope has finished executing. Every function in JavaScript forms a closure — but closures become especially important when a function is returned from another function or passed as a callback, because they keep a live reference to the variables around them. In React, closures are what give event handlers and
callbacks access to the component's current state and props.
When a function is defined, JavaScript creates a closure — a combination of the function itself and the
(all the variables in scope) at the time of definition. That environment is preserved and stays accessible through the function, even after the outer function returns:

This bug is one of the most well-known closure gotchas. Using
in a loop creates a single shared variable that all the closures reference. By the time the callbacks run, the loop is done and the variable holds the final value:

Every function component in React is itself a closure. Each render produces a new scope with the current values of props and state, and all the functions defined in that scope (event handlers, effects) close over those values.
Event Handlers Close Over Current State
The most important React closure pitfall: a closure captures the value of a state variable
at the time it was created
. If state updates later, the old closure still sees the old value. This is called a
A closure is a function that retains access to the variables from its outer scope even after that scope has finished executing.
